concepts.name: Soft Delete Pattern
concepts.explanation: Data is never physically deleted; instead marked inactive
concepts.real_world_usage: Critical for audit logs and recovery

code_snippets.title: Soft Delete Model
code_snippets.code:
class Category(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100, unique=True)
    is_deleted = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    deleted_at = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)

    def soft_delete(self):
        self.is_deleted = True
        self.deleted_at = timezone.now()
        self.save()

    def restore(self):
        self.is_deleted = False
        self.deleted_at = None
        self.save()
code_snippets.notes: This allows restoring categories even after deletion

code_snippets.title: Basic ML Pipeline
code_snippets.code:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

# Load and split data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# Train model
model = LogisticRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Evaluate
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
print(f'Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, predictions):.3f}')
code_snippets.notes: Basic pipeline for any supervised learning task

code_snippets.title: Simple Neural Network with PyTorch
code_snippets.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, output_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.fc1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

# Usage
model = SimpleNN(784, 128, 10)
code_snippets.notes: PyTorch makes implementing neural networks intuitive

topics.title: Transformer Architecture
topics.description: Attention-based architecture revolutionizing NLP and beyond

concepts.name: Attention Mechanism
concepts.explanation: Allows models to focus on relevant parts of input
concepts.real_world_usage: Powers GPT, BERT, and modern language models

code_snippets.title: Multi-Head Attention Implementation
code_snippets.code:
class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_heads):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
    
    def forward(self, query, key, value):
        batch_size = query.size(0)
        Q = self.W_q(query).view(batch_size, -1, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.W_k(key).view(batch_size, -1, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_v(value).view(batch_size, -1, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / (self.d_k ** 0.5)
        attention = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        context = torch.matmul(attention, V)
        return self.W_o(context.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch_size, -1, d_model))

concepts.name: CNN Architecture
concepts.explanation: Feature extraction through local receptive fields

code_snippets.title: CNN for Image Classification
code_snippets.code:
import torch.nn as nn

class CNNClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.features = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),
            nn.Conv2d(64, 128, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.MaxPool2d(2),
            nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1))
        )
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(128, num_classes)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        x = x.view(x.size(0), -1)
        x = self.classifier(x)
        return x
code_snippets.notes: Basic CNN architecture for classification tasks

concepts.name: YOLOv8 Pipeline
concepts.explanation: Real-time object detection with complete workflow
concepts.real_world_usage: Autonomous vehicles, security systems, retail analytics
best_practices: Use transfer learning from pre-trained models

code_snippets.title: YOLOv8 Object Detection
code_snippets.code:
from ultralytics import YOLO

# Load pre-trained model
model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')

# Train on custom dataset
model.train(data='data.yaml', epochs=100, imgsz=640, batch=16)

# Validate
model.val()

# Predict on new images
results = model.predict('test_images/', save=True)

# Export for deployment
model.export(format='onnx')
code_snippets.notes: Complete pipeline using YOLOv8 for object detection

code_snippets.title: Advanced Tokenization with Transformers
code_snippets.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Load tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')

# Tokenize text
text = "Hello, how are you doing today?"
tokens = tokenizer.tokenize(text)
encoded = tokenizer.encode(text, add_special_tokens=True)
decoded = tokenizer.decode(encoded)
print(f'Tokens: {tokens}')

concepts.name: Fine-tuning BERT
concepts.explanation: Adapting pre-trained BERT for specific tasks
concepts.real_world_usage: Sentiment analysis, spam detection, topic classification

code_snippets.title: BERT Fine-tuning Pipeline
code_snippets.code:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

# Load model and tokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased', num_labels=2)

# Encode text
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt', padding=True, truncation=True)
outputs = model(**inputs)
predictions = torch.argmax(outputs.logits, dim=1)
code_snippets.notes: Complete BERT fine-tuning pipeline for text classification

code_snippets.title: Advanced Optimizers Comparison
code_snippets.code:
from torch.optim import SGD, Adam, AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingLR

# Different optimizers
optimizers = {
    'SGD': SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9),
    'Adam': Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001),
    'AdamW': AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, weight_decay=0.01)
}

# Learning rate scheduler
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100)
code_snippets.notes: Modern optimization techniques for stable training

topics.title: FastAPI for ML APIs
topics.description: Building high-performance APIs for ML models

code_snippets.title: FastAPI ML Endpoint
code_snippets.code:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from model import predict

app = FastAPI()

class InputData(BaseModel):
    text: str

@app.post('/predict')
async def predict_sentiment(data: InputData):
    result = predict(data.text)
    return {'prediction': result}

# Run with: uvicorn main:app --reload
code_snippets.notes: FastAPI provides excellent performance for ML APIs

concepts.name: Docker ML Container
concepts.explanation: Packaging ML models with all dependencies
best_practices: Use multi-stage builds for smaller images

code_snippets.title: Dockerfile for ML Application
code_snippets.code:
# Multi-stage build
FROM python:3.9-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

FROM python:3.9-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app /app
COPY model/ ./model/

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
code_snippets.notes: Multi-stage builds reduce image size significantly

concepts.name: ML Pipeline Automation
concepts.real_world_usage: Continuous training, model monitoring, automated retraining

code_snippets.title: ML Pipeline with MLflow
code_snippets.code:
import mlflow
import mlflow.pytorch

# Start MLflow tracking
mlflow.set_tracking_uri('http://localhost:5000')
mlflow.set_experiment('my_experiment')

with mlflow.start_run():
    # Log parameters
    mlflow.log_param('learning_rate', 0.001)
    mlflow.log_param('epochs', 100)
    
    # Train model
    model = train_model()
    
    # Log model
    mlflow.pytorch.log_model(model, 'model')
    
    # Log metrics
    mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', 0.95)

code_snippets.title: K8s Deployment for ML Model
code_snippets.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ml-model-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ml-model
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ml-model
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: ml-model
        image: my-registry/ml-model:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"
code_snippets.notes: HPA enables automatic scaling based on load

concepts.name: Paper Retrieval API
concepts.explanation: Fetching latest papers from arXiv API
concepts.real_world_usage: Staying updated with latest ML research, paper reviews

code_snippets.title: Fetch Latest Papers from arXiv
code_snippets.code:
import arxiv
import feedparser

def get_latest_papers(query='transformer', max_results=5):
    # Using arxiv library
    search = arxiv.Search(
        query=query,
        max_results=max_results,
        sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.SubmittedDate
    )
    
    papers = []
    for result in search.results():
        papers.append({
            'title': result.title,
            'authors': [a.name for a in result.authors],
            'summary': result.summary,
            'published': result.published,
            'url': result.pdf_url
        })
    
    return papers

# Alternative: Using feedparser for RSS
def get_papers_via_rss(category='cs.LG', max_items=10):
    url = f'http://export.arxiv.org/api/query?search_cat={category}&start=0&max_results={max_items}'
    feed = feedparser.parse(url)
    return [(entry.title, entry.summary, entry.link) for entry in feed.entries]
code_snippets.notes: arXiv API provides access to latest ML research papers

concepts.name: Hugging Face API Integration
concepts.real_world_usage: Discovering new tools, staying current with state-of-the-art
best_practices: Follow model cards for best practices

code_snippets.title: Fetch Trending Models from Hugging Face
code_snippets.code:
import requests

def get_trending_models(limit=10):
    """Fetch trending models from Hugging Face"""
    url = f'https://huggingface.co/api/models?sort=downloads&direction=-1&limit={limit}'
    response = requests.get(url)
    
    if response.status_code == 200:
        models = response.json()
        return [
            {
                'model_id': m['modelId'],
                'downloads': m['downloads'],
                'likes': m['likes'],
                'pipeline_tag': m.get('pipeline_tag', 'N/A')
            }
            for m in models
        ]
    return []

def get_model_card(model_id):
    """Fetch model card/readme content"""
    url = f'https://huggingface.co/api/models/{model_id}'
    response = requests.get(url)
    return response.json() if response.status_code == 200 else None
code_snippets.notes: Hugging Face API provides access to trending models and datasets
